observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following:
The Municipal Accountant to exert all efforts to reconcile and analyze the accounts to determine whether they warrant adjustment in accordance with existing COA rules and regulations;
The Municipal Accountant submits to the Audit Team a certification and/or reasons why the books of accounts/records/financial statements/schedules and supporting documents cannot be located; and
The Management requires proper turnover of documents/pending work from officials and employees retiring/separating from office/service to ensure completeness of records.

We recommended that the Management:
assess the collectability of the accounts and send demand letters to the concerned individuals or their heir/s; and
request for write-off of those accounts falling under the dormant receivables as defined by COA Circular No. 2016-005 by completing the required documents under the same COA circular.

We recommended that the Management adopt strict measures to regulate the granting of cash advances in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular 97-002 dated February 10, 1997. Any unused or unspent cash advances should be returned or refunded.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly monitor the settlement of unliquidated cash advances and discontinue granting additional cash advances unless all outstanding cash advances have been liquidated. Failure to liquidate the cash advances within the prescribed period shall constitute a valid cause to withhold the salary of the grantee.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant exert effort in determining the details of the recorded audit disallowances, and disclose in the Notes to Financial Statement the history/nature of the recorded audit disallowances.

We recommended that the Management, through the Municipal Accountant and the Inventory Committee, regularly reconcile their reports so that any discrepancy noted can be promptly adjusted and reconciled in the books of the Municipality.

We recommended the following:
The Management to exert efforts to locate all necessary documents and pursue the titling of the remaining parcel(s) of land in the name of the Municipality; and
The Property Officer and Municipal Accountant to regularly conduct reconciliation of their reports so that any discrepancy noted can be promptly adjusted and reconciled.

We recommended that:
the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer regularly reconcile their records to determine the status and correct balances of the aforementioned CIP accounts;
the Municipal Accountant to transfer the completed projects from the CIP accounts to the appropriate PPE accounts, as well as to compute the required depreciation on such projects and accordingly adjust the balances of the affected accounts; and
The cost of the constructed Multi-Purpose Hall must be transferred to the DepEd Division Office of Nueva Vizcaya through a Deed of Donation and be derecognized in the books of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management fast-track the implementation of the projects for which the downloaded funds are intended, and fully disburse the balances of the payables under the Trust Fund in accordance with the specific purpose stated in the trust agreement or the Memorandum of Agreement (MOA). If its implementation is no longer possible, return the balances to the respective trustor.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile and analyze the accounts to determine whether they warrant reversion of the amount in accordance with existing COA rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that its disclosure fully complies with the provisions of Section 129 of IPSAS 1 and Section 36 of IPSAS 19, and explicitly identify whether any contingent liabilities exist in the Notes to FSs.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to observe the disclosure requirements in the preparation of the financial statements in accordance with IPSAS Nos. 1 and 20 to ensure that the entity is transparent about its dealings with related parties and provide users with additional information in understanding the financial statements and aid in the decision-making as well as achieve fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended that the Management, through the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer:
determine the cause of the discrepancies between the monthly remittances/refunds against the withholdings, and take the necessary action, whether to remit the difference or refund the same to the employees, whichever is appropriate; and
ensure that all taxes withheld during the month are remitted in their entirety on or before the date prescribed under Section 5 of RR No. 11-208 dated January 31, 2018.

We recommended that the Management, through the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer:
determine the cause of the discrepancies between the monthly remittances/refunds against the withholdings, and take the necessary action, whether to remit the difference or refund the same to the employees, whichever is appropriate; and
ensure that all contributions withheld during the month shall be remitted in their entirety on or before the date prescribed under Section 6 (b) of RA No. 8291 and Section 3 of the IRR of RA No. 9679, respectively.

We recommended the following courses of action:
The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the balances of the Due to PhilHealth account and, upon determination, remit the balances to the concerned agency or refund the same to the concerned individuals, whichever is appropriate; and
The Management to ensure that all contributions withheld during the month shall be remitted in its entirety on or before the date prescribed under PhilHealth Circular No. 001, s. 2014 dated January 09, 2014.

We recommended that the Management, through the Municipal DRRM Officer and other concerned implementing offices, fully utilize the available appropriations and implement all budgeted projects, programs and activities within the planned period of completion to fully achieve their intended purposes.

We recommended that the Management strictly observe the prescribed policy on the granting of financial assistance out of the QRF. We further recommended that the MDRRMO ensure that each use and release of QRF in the future is supported with a Local Sanggunian Resolution for such a purpose.

We therefore reiterated our previous recommendation that the Management, through the LGU GFPS, prepare the GAD Agenda and use it as the basis for the annual formulation of more gender-responsive PPAs to be included in the LGU GPB pursuant to Section 4.1.C.2.1 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01.

We recommended that only those programs and activities that are responsive to the gender issues identified as a result of the formulation of the GAD Agenda shall be included in the GPB and Accomplishment Reports.

We recommended that the Management ensure that payroll deductions shall be limited following the order of preference provided in the GAA and ensure that an employee’s net take-home pay shall not be less than the minimum allowed take-home pay as provided under the provisions of the General Appropriations Act.
We also recommended that the Management hold the withholding agents responsible for allowing the net take-home pay of the employees to be less than that allowed by the GAA.

We recommended that the Management enforce the strict collection of the delinquent RPT/SET and formulate an effective strategy or policy to compel the taxpayers to pay their taxes actively and on time pursuant to the provisions of Section 249 of the Local Government Code of the Philippines.

We recommended that the Management thru the Municipal Accountant strictly adhere to the prescribed time frame for the submission of accounts to the Auditor concerned pursuant to Section 39 (1) (3) of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445 and Section 23 (1) (3), Subtitle B, Title I, Book V of the Administrative Code of 1987 to enable us to make a timely decision in the audit.
We recommended that the Management ensure that the assets of the agency, especially cash and records pertaining thereto, are protected and properly safeguarded by providing an appropriate safe/vault.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee ensure that the performance security is posted by the winning bidder within the prescribed period and in the correct type, form, wording and validity period pursuant to Section 39.1 of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee ensure that the warranty security is posted by the contractor within the prescribed period and in the correct type, form, wording, validity period and amount. Likewise, we recommended that the Management require the contractor to post a supplemental Warranty Security for the completed project pursuant to the provisions of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere to the prescribed time frame for the submission of the Financial Statements to the Auditor concerned pursuant to Section 41 of P.D. No. 1445 or the Government Auditing Code of the Philippines to enable us to make a timely decision in the audit.

We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere to the prescribed time frame for the submission of accounts to the Auditor concerned pursuant to Section 39 (1) (3) of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445 and Section 23 (1) (3), Subtitle B, Title I, Book V of The Administrative Code of 1987 to enable us to make a timely decision in the audit. Likewise, submit immediately the disbursement vouchers and supporting documents to the Auditor within the prescribed period to avoid suspension/disallowance in the audit.
We recommended that the management ensure that the assets of the agency especially cash and records pertaining thereto are protected and properly safeguarded by providing an appropriate safe/vault.

We recommended that the Management ensure daily and intact remittances of collections of the Collecting Officers pursuant to Sections 101 and 105 of PD 1445 and Section 29 of the New Government Accounting System Manual for LGUs.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended management to:
Strictly comply with the provisions of pertinent accounting and auditing rules and regulations on the preparation of bank reconciliation statements particularly COA Circular No. 96-11 dated October 2, 1996; and
The Municipal Accounting Office make necessary adjustments for all reconciling items presented in the bank reconciliation statements, such as, debit memoranda (DMs), stale checks and error in recording during the period these were identified in order to reflect the correct balance of the Cash in Bank account in any accounting period.

We recommended the Accounting Office to transfer the completed assets totaling P29,540,562.04 to their particular asset accounts in compliance with Sections 237 and 251 (g) of the Government Accounting Manual (GAM) for LGUs.

We recommended the Accounting Office to make the necessary adjustments to reflect the depreciations of the PPEs totaling P44,348,239.42 in the books in compliance with Section 262 (b) of the Government Accounting Manual (GAM) for LGUs. We further recommended the accounting office to record monthly depreciations for all of the property, plant and equipment of the municipality in compliance with Section 2.1 of COA Circular Letter No. 2004-003.

We recommended the Accounting Office to adjust the infrastructure assets totaling to P20,221,28279 to their proper infrastructure asset accounts and to ensure that all infrastructure assets acquired in the future will be classified and recorded to their proper accounts in compliance with Section 218 of the Government Accounting Manual (GAM) for LGUs Volume I.

We recommended that management identify the source of the unreconciled balance between the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds through reconciliation of the accounts and immediately reflect any adjustments.

We recommended the Accounting Office to properly record the transactions in accordance with the descriptions provided by the Government Accounting Manual (GAM) Volume II. We further recommend the adjustment of the advances which were erroneously recorded as Due from Officers and Employees.

We recommended that the Accounting Office make the necessary adjustments in the books of the Municipality to present fairly the balances of the Financial Statements accounts, specifically, the Non-Current Liabilities in compliance with the provisions of Paragraph 80 of the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards (PPSAS) No. 1.

We recommended the management:
to record all its transactions by using the proper accounts in accordance with the Government Accounting Manual (GAM) for LGUs Volume III;
to record the funds given to NGAs, other LGUs, NGOs/POs, and Leagues for specific purposes as receivables and required the implementing agencies to liquidate the same; and
to adjust the erroneous recordings.

We recommended management to refrain from its practice of making direct payment of financial assistance to officials/employees of the operating units/stations of the national government agencies within the Municipality. The Municipality may either make the payment directly to the supplier or transfer funds to these concerned offices/stations in accordance with the budget appropriated by the LGU to their offices but these shall be accounted as Due from National Government Agencies and the recipient offices/stations have the obligation to liquidate their expenditures to the Municipality.

We recommended:
the management to submit the required documentary requirements prescribed under Item 3 of COA Circular No. 2012-001;
the Accounting Office to make the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balances of accounts; and
the Implementing Agency should be advised to submit the required utilization report / liquidation documents as proof that the fund transferred to them was used for its intended purpose, otherwise, the amount of P230,000.00 will remain as receivable by the Municipality from the Implementing Agency until the latter liquidate the same.

We recommended that the Accounting Office maintain all the required ledgers in compliance with the provision of Section 4 (i) of the Government Accounting Manual (GAM) for LGUs, Volume I, to ensure that all transactions were properly documented and recorded and the balances appearing on the financial statements are accurate and reliable.  We further recommended the filing of appropriate administrative/criminal action against erring officials and employees, in case of deliberate none compliance with the establishment and maintenance of subsidiary ledgers for all of the accounts of the municipality.

We recommended that:
the Disbursing Officer liquidate all his cash advances at the end of each year as required under Section 5.8 of COA Circular No. 97-002;
the management enforce immediate liquidation of cash advances which are due for liquidations especially those which are long overdue in accordance with Sections 5.7 and 5.8 of COA Circular No. 97-002; and
the management resort legal remedies to enforce liquidation or settlement in the case of those which are no longer in the service.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive enforce settlement of the above audit disallowances that has become final and executory in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006. Also, identify the source of P30.00 difference.

We recommended the management to refrain from granting benefits without legal basis. Refund of the granted Economic Resiliency Allowances (ERA) and Calamity and Economic Resiliency Subsidies (CERS) is also enjoined, otherwise, these will be disallowed in audit.

We recommended management to direct the concerned offices/employees to discontinue the practice of forging or falsifying signatures and stamps or allowing others to do the same and strictly comply with the provisions of the Revised Penal Code of the Philippines and the Government Procurement Reform Act, otherwise, refer the filing of criminal and administrative cases against the persons liable to the Office of Ombudsman, if warranted.

We recommended that management strictly observe the provisions of Section 48.2 and Annex H of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184. We further recommend the imposition of appropriate disciplinary action to persons liable as provided under Section 70 of the same IRR if warranted.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the management post Notices of Award, Contracts and Notices to Proceed for all procurements undertaken through public bidding in the PhilGEPS to comply with the provisions of Section 37 and Annex H of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of RA No. 9184 to promote transparency and efficiency through modernization, standardization and regulation of procurement activities.

We recommended that management direct the Municipal Accounting and Treasury Offices to immediately submit the Disbursement Voucher and supporting documents of the Check No. 1033718 amounting P977,001.00 to the Auditor for verification and audit, pursuant to afore-cited law and regulations. Henceforth, strictly observe the prescribed period in the submission of accounts to avoid suspensions/disallowances in audit.

We recommended that the Accounting Office submit and attach all the necessary documentary requirements in all disbursements of the municipality as provided under Section 4 (6) of P.D. No. 1445 and the guidelines set forth by COA Circular No. 2012-001. We further recommend that all disbursement vouchers and supporting documents shall be properly accomplished and signed before releasing the payment. Failure to comply with the said recommendation shall result to the issuance of Notice of Suspension which shall mature into disallowance if unsettled after 90 days from receipt thereof.

We recommended the management to deliver all the reported donated items to the barangays, refrain from making untruthful statements of donations and avoid the acts which will lead to misappropriation of public funds or property.

We recommended that management:
study the flow of the transaction documents from inception, processing, authorization and payment of financial assistance and based thereon, set up clear-cut policies and guidelines on its program of granting financial assistances, donations and other form of assistances which will be in line with DSWD MC No. 2014-002; and
define the qualifications of the beneficiaries, amounts to be given, and the processing office to ensure that only qualified beneficiaries are assisted and that the assistance is given only for public and general welfare purposes.

We recommended that management enforce the strict collection of the delinquent RPT/SET and formulate an effective strategy or policy to compel the taxpayers to pay their taxes actively and on time pursuant to the provisions of Section 249 of the Local Government Code of the Philippines.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere with the provisions of Section 271 (3)(d) of the Local Government Code and release promptly the shares of the barangays in the municipal real property and community tax collections to augment the finances of the affected local government units. We further recommend that the Accounting Office maintain subsidiary ledgers for each barangay for proper monitoring of balances due to them.

We recommended Management to:
strengthen its internal control;
ensure that all financial reports and forms are properly and completely accomplished to guaranty the propriety and regularity of its transactions; and
adopt the forms provided by the Government Accounting Manual (GAM) for LGUs Volume II.

We recommended that the Disbursing Officer shall stamp “PAID” the disbursement vouchers and all of the corresponding supporting documents after payment and indicate the check number and date of actual payment on each “Paid” stamp to avert any attempt to use the same supporting documents in future claims, pursuant to COA Circular No. 92-389 dated November 3, 1992 thereby safeguarding government funds against loss.

We recommended:
the accountable officers to restitute their shortages to avoid any initiation of administrative or criminal actions against the them;
the Liquidating Officer to ensure that all collections of the collecting officers are remitted daily and intact, otherwise, she will be held liable together with the collecting officers for deliberately overlooking the irregular acts of the latter; and
the Municipal Treasurer, in his supervising capacity, to ensure that all accountable forms were properly monitored to avoid unreported/unremitted collections.

We recommended:
the accountable officers to record correctly all collections and deposits/remittances in the cashbooks in compliance with the sound recording of government transactions and with the provisions of COA Circular No. 92-382 to ensure that all received funds are duly accounted for and their disposition are properly monitored;
the accountable officers to record the transactions in the prescribed cashbook in chronological order or daily as prescribed in Section 63 (A)(1) and (D) of the Local Treasury Operation Manual (LTOM) in order to proper monitor their respective daily accountabilities; and
the Liquidating Officer and Accounting Office reconcile the balances between the cashbooks and accounting records as required under Section 63 (F) of the LTOM to ensure the accuracy of balances appearing in the financial reports.

We recommended:
the Liquidating Officer to ensure that all of the collections of the collecting officers were remitted to her daily and intact; and
the imposition of administrative and/or criminal action against the erring accountable officers if the said irregular practice was not discontinued.
We recommended the Municipal Treasurer to stop the issuance of accountable forms to unappointed and unbonded individual in compliance with Section 59(Q) of the Local Treasury Operation Manual (LTOM).

We recommended the Disbursing Officer to properly monitor and account his cash accountability in compliance with the provisions of Sections 101 and 105 of PD No. 1445, and to ensure that all of his cash advances will be liquidated within the prescribed period.

We recommended the Disbursing Officer to maintain all necessary cashbooks for each cash advances under different funds and record the transactions in the cashbook in chronological order in compliance with the provisions of Section 6 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Section 32 of the Government Accounting Manual (GAM) for LGU Volume II.

We recommended the Disbursing Officer stop the act of transferring cash advances to other officer or employee to avoid misappropriation of government fund as mandated under Section 4.1.6 of COA Circular No. 97-002. The Disbursing Officer shall be warned that she will be held liable for any loss of fund under her accountability as a result of her negligence.

We recommended that management implement all programmed projects during the year these were legislated and approved to allow early utilization by the beneficiaries thereof. Likewise, monitoring and assessment by the Local Finance Committee should also be undertaken to ensure timely completion of these projects.

We recommended that management fully utilize the 80% of its share from national wealth for programs and projects intended to lower the costs of electricity of their qualified constituents to alleviate their current situations and to adhere the provisions of Section 294 of the Republic Act No. 7160, otherwise known as the Local Government Code of the Philippines. We further recommend that Section 3 (a) of Part B of Republic Act No. 9136 be considered in drawing up programs and activities to be funded from the 80% component of the Municipality’s share from national wealth.

We recommended that management, thought the LGU GAD Focal Point System, ensure the full implementation of all identified GAD programs/projects/activities to attain the GAD objectives of pursuing gender equality and empowerment. We further recommend the establishment of LGU GAD monitoring and evaluation system to ensure the effective and efficient implementation of GAD plans and programs as mandated by PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting journal entry to take up the unutilized balance by debiting Prior Year Adjustments and crediting the Due to NGAS account. Henceforth, succeeding receipts of the fund, if any, shall be recorded as liability under Due to NGAs in conformity with GAM for LGU.

We recommended that the management fully comply with posting and reporting requirements of the Bayanihan Grant to Cities and Municipalities (BGCM) per Section 4.3 of DMB Local Budget Circular No. 125 dated April 7, 2020.

We recommended management to submit the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances for CY 2020 and elaborate its internal procedures and protocols during the distribution of donations to beneficiaries. Starting year 2021 and until the quarantine status is lifted, regularly submit the required report on in-kind donations pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-009.

We recommended the management to submit the report of when the food assistances were delivered, the inventory of the items given, and inventory undistributed items, if any, pursuant to Sections 2 and 4 of PD No. 1445, for audit verification, otherwise the disbursement without complete supporting documents will be suspended in audit.

We recommended the management to submit the lacking documents to be evaluated in audit. We further recommend the strict compliance with the documentary requirements of procurement under emergency purchase while responding to the needs brought by the pandemic.

We recommended the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to explain in writing its reason for non-compliance to the posting requirement of the GPPB and elaborate other means of communicating information to the public on the awarded contracts through the emergency procurements under the Bayanihan Act. We further recommend the BAC to stay updated with the GPPB issuances and strictly adhere to its policies.
We recommended the Accounting Office to record the biological assets in the books to reflect the correct total assets of the municipality in accordance with Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009.
